1.text: Rhinogobius biwaensis, a new gobiid fish of the “yoshinobori” species complex, Rhinogobius spp., endemic to Lake Biwa, Japan

The endemic lentic goby of Lake Biwa in Japan, which has hitherto been designated informally as Rhinogobius sp. BW, is formally described as Rhinogobius biwaensis sp. nov. It is distinguished from other members of the genus by the following combination of characters: vertebral count 26 (rarely 27); pectoral fin rays 18–22 (mostly 20–21); longitudinal scales 31–36 (mostly 31–34, mode 32); predorsal scales absent or few; D1 low with non-filamentous spines, all of almost same length except for shorter 1st and 6th (last) spines in both sexes; pelvic fin disk composed of thin membranes, its frenum undeveloped with a low, thin lamella; cheek unmarked; back and sides of body with 5–6 unclear dark saddles and 6–9 dark blotches; D1 lacking blotches but melanized to greater or lesser degree along rays; dorsal edge of pectoral base with one small, dark spot tinged with blue metallic luster; in breeding males, yellow on suboperculum and branchiostegal region and on basal part of anal fin, blue on medial part of under jaw.
This article was registered in the Official Register of Zoological Nomenclature (ZooBank) as 1ACAD0D2-9936-45D1-A236-55E6681BF984.
This article was published as an Online First article on the online publication date shown on this page. The article should be cited by using the doi number.
Rhinogobius biwaensis · Gobiidae · Lake Biwa · Japan

Rhinogobius Gill 1859 fishes are freshwater gobies that are distributed widely in East and Southeast Asia. They show a high degree of regional differentiation and comprise more than 86 species (Table 1), including the new species described herein (Wu and Zhong 2008; Akihito et al. 2013; Suzuki et al. 2015; Eschmeyer 2017). Apart from Rhinogobius similis Gill 1859, the Japanese species of this genus are very closely related and form a species complex that used to be lumped under the Japanese name “yoshinobori”. Rhinogobius similis, which was proved by Suzuki et al. (2015) to be a senior synonym of Rhinogobius giurinus (Rutter 1897), is the only species of Rhinogobius from Japan that does not belong to this “yoshinobori” species complex. The members of this “yoshinobori” species complex are referred to in this paper as “yoshinobori” species or “yoshinobori” fishes. At least 16 “yoshinobori” species are currently recognized in various parts of Japan, though nine of them have no scientific name as yet.

The genus Rhinogobius was erected by Gill (1859) when he described Rhinogobius similis from Japan. Afterwards, Lake Biwa was counted as one of its habitats as a result of confusion concerning this genus, and the name “yoshinobori” was cited as one of Japanese local names of R. similis (see Jordan and Snyder 1901a; Jordan and Snyder 1901b: 56–58; Jordan et al. 1913: 341–342). Tomiyama (1936: 68–70) lumped the more than ten nominal species that had been described by then and considered them to represent one polymorphic species, “yoshinobori”. As a result of reexamination of the type specimen, Gobius brunneus

Temminck and Schlegel (1845: 142–143) proved to belong to Rhinogobius, and R. similis came to be considered a junior synonym of the Rhinogobius brunneus (see Koumans 1935: 149; Boeseman 1947: 123; Takagi 1962; but see Suzuki et al. 2015). Rhinogobius brunneus (Temminck and Schlegel 1845) thus was then, for a while, treated as the valid scientific name of the polymorphic species “yoshinobori” in Japan.

Mizuno (1960a, b) then found a goby fish resembling then R. brunneus but laying larger eggs, and he described it as a new species, Tukugobius flumineus Mizuno 1960, which became Rhinogobius flumineus (Mizuno 1960) because Herre (1933) had already declared that Tukugobius Herre is a junior synonym of Rhinogobius Gill. Thereafter some color-pattern morphs and egg-size morphs of R. brunneus were recognized one after another. Studies on ecology, ethology, and molecular genetics eventually revealed that most of those morphs represent distinct species (Mizuoka 1967, 1968, 1974, 1978; Nishijima 1968; Nakayama 1975; Mizuno 1976, 1981; Uehara 1980; Iwata 1981; Mizuno et al. 1982; Masuda et al. 1989; Suzuki 1992, 1996; Katoh and Nishida 1994). Those Tomiyama (1936: 68–70) had lumped together were subdivided again.

An effort to assign formal scientific names is ongoing (Oijen et al. 2011; Suzuki and Chen 2011), though morphological similarity among taxa, lost or ambiguous type material, and insufficient taxonomic descriptions have made it difficult to complete the task. The undescribed species were provisionally named with two-capital-letter alphabetic designations (Mizuno et al. 1989).

Akihito et al. (2000) identified 11 “yoshinobori” species in Japan: R. flumineus, Rhinogobius sp. DA, Rhinogobius sp. CB, Rhinogobius sp. LD, Rhinogobius sp. BI, Rhinogobius sp. MO, Rhinogobius sp. DL, Rhinogobius sp. CO, Rhinogobius sp. YB, Rhinogobius sp. BB, Rhinogobius sp. OR. Among them, Rhinogobius sp. OR was considered to be a species having much variation, including four morphs: “Toshoku”, “Gi-toshoku”, “Shinjiko”, and “Shimahire”.

After that, by morphological comparison with the type specimen, one of the “yoshinobori” species complex called Rhinogobius sp. DA was shown to actually be R. brunneus (Temminck and Schlegel 1845) (see Oijen et al. 2011). Detailed reinvestigation of morphology by Suzuki and Chen (2011) also suggested that Rhinogobius sp. CB, the “Gi-toshoku” morph of Rhinogobius sp. OR, and Rhinogobius sp. LD are actually the nominal species Rhinogobius nagoyae Jordan and Seale 1906, Rhinogobius kurodai (Tanaka 1908), and Rhinogobius fluviatilis Tanaka 1925, respectively. Suzuki et al. (2012) described Rhinogobius sp. BI formally as Rhinogobius ogasawaraensis Suzuki, Chen and Senou 2012.

Akihito et al. (2013) then split Rhinogobius sp. OR sensu Akihito et al. (2000) into six species on the basis of preceding works. The lake-river migrating population of Lake Biwa, representative of “Toshoku” morph, was considered a distinct species, Rhinogobius sp. OM. The morph “Gi-toshoku” included two species: R. kurodai, which lives in lentic water bodies in Tokyo and Kanagawa Prefectures (Suzuki and Chen 2011), and Rhinogobius sp. KZ from the Kazusa hilly area of Chiba Prefecture (Uehara 1980). The “Shimahire” morph mainly distributed in stagnant freshwaters around the Seto Inland Sea was elevated to a distinct species, Rhinogobius sp. BF (Ohara et al. 2009; Suzuki et al. 2010). In addition, the two lentic populations from Lake Biwa (Takahashi 1990, 1997; Takahashi and Okazaki 2002; Takahashi and Ohara 2004) and from the Nobi-Okazaki Plain in the Nagoya area (Nishimura 2000; Suzuki and Sakamoto 2005), which had been treated as variations of the “Shimahire” morph, were recognized as two different species, respectively: Rhinogobius sp. BW (described herein as Rhinogobius biwaensis sp. nov.) and Rhinogobius sp. TO. As a result, the number of recognized, albeit often undescribed, “Yoshinobori” species in Japan has reached 16 (Akihito et al. 2013). This left the morph “Shinjiko”, some populations included in the morph “Gi-toshoku”, and some other unclassified

5.text: populations that had been included in Rhinogobius sp. OR sensu Akihito et al. (2000) unresolved taxonomically. On the other hand, in the recent studies of the genus, R. kurodai is compared with Rhinogobius sp. BF and rediagnosed by Suzuki et al. (in press).

Takahashi and Okazaki (2002) proposed to divide the “yoshinobori” species into four groups depending on their life history patterns, namely amphidromous, fluvial, lake-river migrating, and lentic fishes. Amphidromy is the original lifestyle of the “yoshinobori” species complex: larvae hatch in a river and drift down immediately to the sea, feed and grow there, come back into the river at a young-fish stage, then grow some more and reproduce in the river. The fluvial fish is non-diadromous and completes its life cycle by laying its comparatively large eggs in the upper reaches of rivers. The lake-river migrating lifestyle resembles amphidromous lifestyle: fish migrates between lake and its inflowing rivers, substituting the lake for the sea. The lentic fish lives in a lake or pond and does not undertake lake-river migrations. The life styles of latter three must cause genetic isolation more easily than amphidromous life style. In Lake Biwa, an ancient lake in Japan, there inhabit not only a lake–river migrating species, Rhinogobius sp. OM, but also lentic species, Rhinogobius sp. BW. In the present study, we belatedly provide a formal description of Rhinogobius sp. BW, the lentic “yoshinobori” species in Lake Biwa.

The holotype, LBM 1210013432, was caught in a tradi-tional “eri” fish trap (Hashimoto 1999, 2015:373) at a depth of approximately 15 m off Wanihama beach on the southwestern shore of Lake Biwa in what is now Wani-nakahama, Otsu City, on 16 July 1997, and fixed at the sites in 10% formalin (i.e. 3.5–3.8% formaldehyde). Two of 78 paratypes, OMNH-P 23927 and OMNH-P 23932, were caught by hand net at Ukawa, Takashima City on 25 June 2011, and photographed while they were fresh, and fixed in 10% formalin. The remaining 76 paratypes, which were caught in either dragnets or “eri” fish trap, were fixed at the sites in 10% formalin.

Measurements, counts, and observations of fixed speci-mens were made under a binocular microscope following Hubbs and Lagler (1964) and Akihito et al. (2013). Mea-surements (to the nearest 0.1 mm) were made on the entire type series. Standard length, snout length, and head length were measured from the tip of the upper lip. Bones, fin rays, and scales of 41 paratype specimens were examined after staining them with sodium alizarinsulfonate (Kawa-mura and Hosoya 1991). The holotype and 37 other para-types were not stained to avoid damage to them. Bones of

the holotype were examined using soft X-ray by “M-60” apparatus of SOFTEX Co., Ltd., and its fin rays and scales were examined without staining. Cephalic sensory canal system of the holotype and three paratypes were examined. The observation methods and the names used for parts of the cephalic sensory canal system followed Akihito et al. (2013). Some specimens of nameless unclassified popula-tions of Rhinogobius that were compared morphologically with the new species are shown in Table 2. Their predorsal scales were counted without staining.

Abbreviations and locations of depositories of speci-mens are as follows: AMS (The Australian Museum, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia), FAKU (The Kyoto University Museum, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto, Japan), LBM (Lake Biwa Museum, Kusatsu, Shiga, Japan), NSMT (National Museum of Nature and Science, Tsukuba, Ibaraki, Japan), OMNH (Osaka Museum of Natural History, Higashi-Su-miyoshi-ku, Osaka, Japan). Scientific names, Japanese names, and provisional two-capital-letter alphabetic des-ignations of “yoshinobori” species in Japan follow Akihito et al. (2000, 2013).
Rhinogobius brunneus: Takahashi 1990: 225 (Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan).
Biwa-yoshinobori (tentative name): Takahashi 1997: 128 (Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan).

Rhinogobius sp. BW: Takahashi and Okazaki 2002: 333 (Hamabun, Maiko, Shimosakamoto, Misaki and Biwa-cho, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Tsujimoto et al. 2003: 17 (Omi-Maiko, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Takahashi and Ohara 2004: 100 (Ukawa shore and Minamihama, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Senou et al. 2004: 459 (Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Suzuki et al. 2010: 11 (Ukawa, Lake Biwa, Shiga; a pond, Watarai, Mie; Yato R., Gotsu, Shimane; and Taniyama R., Imabari, Ehime; Japan); Mukai et al. 2012: 18 (Sugaura and Kitakomatsu, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Akihito et al. 2013: 1461 (secondary sources); Yamasaki et al. 2015: 23 (Imajyuku and Moriyama, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan); Mukai et al. 2015: 175 (Ogasatakahashi R., Kikugawa, Shizuoka; Asuri Dam Reservoir, Toyota, Aichi; Yokoyama Dam Reservoir, Ibigawa, Gifu; a pond, Watarai, Mie; Yubara Dam Reservoir, Maniwa, Okayama, Yato R., Gotsu, Shimane; and Taniyama R., Imabari, Ehime; Japan). Fujita and Uchiyama 2015: 447 (Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan; and secondary sources).
